title-group.article-title: Lingual Papillae in Hamdani Sheep During Foetal Periods: Gross, Scanning Electron Microscopy, Histochemical and Immunohistochemical Analysis

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: This study aimed to investigate the developmental, macroscopic, microscopic and immunohistochemical characteristics of tongue tissues in sheep foetuses collected at three gestational periods. The study was carried out with foetuses collected from pregnant ewes slaughtered in private slaughterhouses. For this purpose, a total of 15 foetuses, 5 each from the first trimester (38–48 days), second trimester (56–73 days) and third trimester (105–118 days), were used. Foetal tongues were carefully dissected and removed from the oral cavity. First, their macroscopic properties were examined using a stereomicroscope. Scanning electron microscopy was used for microscopic morphological evaluations, and the tongues were examined under a light microscope using Haematoxylin Eosin and Masson Trichrome methods. The expression and localization of cytokeratins (CK)‐5, E‐cadherin, EphB2 and EphB3 proteins in the tongue epithelium were determined by immunohistochemical analysis. According to the results of light and electron microscopy, it was determined that the tongue tissue began to develop in the first trimester, and lingual papillae could be seen as primitive primordia. Although the tongue continued to grow in the second trimester, primitive taste buds and papilla structures were detected. In the last trimester, lingual papillae were observed to become more prominent. In addition to taste buds, serous glands in the papillae were also detected. It was determined that both taste and mechanical papillae formed simultaneously during the development of lingual papillae, and the papillae were located at the apex, body and root without a chronological order. Furthermore, epithelial markers such as CK‐5 and E‐cadherin as well as EphB2 and EphB3 receptor expressions were detected in the epithelium covering the papillae in all three trimesters. In addition to contributing to the morphological literature, our results may provide information for future studies aimed at elucidating the normal development of the tongue and other epithelial tissues in sheep.
*First Trimester: Tongue development began, and lingual papillae appeared as primordium.
*Second Trimester: The tongue grew, and primitive taste buds and papilla structures formed.
*Third Trimester: Lingual papillae became prominent, and taste buds and serous glands were detected.
*Taste and mechanical papillae developed simultaneously without a chronological order.
*CK‐5, E‐cadherin, EphB2 and EphB3 proteins were expressed in the epithelium in all trimesters.

institution-wrap.institution: Türkiye Bilimsel ve Teknolojik Araştırma Kurumu

fn.p: Funding: This research was supported by Türkiye Bilimsel ve Teknolojik Araştırma Kurumu.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: In mammals, the tongue is an important organ of the digestive system located in the oral cavity, consisting of striated muscles, adipose tissue, connective tissue and glands. The tongue, which is a mobile organ, performs many functions such as food intake, liquid absorption, licking, hard food selection, sucking, tasting, chewing, swallowing, touching and regulation of hair (Bahadır and Yıldız 2016). Its morphological diversity varies greatly according to dietary habits (Mistretta 1999). It consists of three separate parts: apex, body and root. The dorsal part of the tongue facing the palate is called the dorsum. In ruminant animals, there is a bulge called a torus at the back of the dorsum, and a fossa called the lingual fossa is observed just in front of the torus linguae. The tongue mucosa bears different papillae, located in different regions and in different numbers, with gustatory or mechanistic roles that aid the specific feeding mechanism of each animal species (Kandyel et al. 2021; Kurtul and Atalgin 2008; Mahdy et al. 2021). Although mechanical papillae are responsible for carrying out mechanical tasks such as grinding, gustatory papillae are responsible for taste perception. Mechanical papillae are classified as filiform papillae, conic papillae and lentiform (lenticularis) papillae, whereas gustatory papillae are divided into fungiform papillae, vallate (circumvallate) papillae and foliate papillae (Sisson and Grossman 1975; Dyce et al. 1996). There are variations in the topographic distribution of lingual papillae types (Kandyel et al. 2021).

In the scientific literature, several articles have been published on the morphology of lingual papillae in various animal species (Can et al. 2016; İlgün et al. 2020; Cizek et al. 2020) and humans (Witt and Reutter 1997; Dursun et al. 2020), as well as on the scanning electron microscopy of the three‐dimensional structures of lingual papillae (Haligur et al. 2019; Demirci et al. 2023).

Cytokeratins (CK) are keratin proteins found in the cytoskeleton of epithelial tissue. They are an essential component of intermediate filaments that help cells resist mechanical stress (Fuertes et al. 2013). CK‐5 is expressed in the epidermis and the multilayered epithelium covering the skin and digestive tract and is a marker of multilayered differentiation (Morgan and Su 1994; Kim et al. 2016). E‐cadherin, a member of calcium (Ca2+)‐dependent cell adhesion proteins, is an essential component of intracellular cell–cell adhesions (adhesion junctions) between contacting cells in epithelial tissues (Takeichi 1991). The cytokeratin family and E‐cadherin expression in the developing sheep tongue epithelium are unknown.

Eph receptors constitute the largest family of receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs). There are two main subtypes: EphA and EphB. Because their ligands, ephrins, are membrane‐bound and require direct cell–cell contact, the complementary expression of EphBs is required in many tissues during development to separate intermingled cells and maintain sharp boundaries (Batlle and Wilkinson 2012; Kullander and Klein 2002). The expression levels and partial roles of EphB receptors in neural development (Altick et al. 2005), intestinal development (Islam et al. 2010) and vasculogenesis (Adams et al. 1999), as well as in the developmental processes of oral structures such as palatogenesis (Risley et al. 2009), have been partially reported. However, the expression of tongue papillae in any mammal has not been described during any process of intrauterine development.

Any disorder in the normal development of the tongue or anomalies (such as cleft tongue, bifid tongue, macroglossia, microglossia and ankyloglossia) that may prevent the normal function of the tongue in postnatal life can be observed (Moore 1982). Therefore, prenatal studies of the tongue are important for understanding normal development.

Sheep in the subgroup Ruminantia constitute more than half of the total number of small ruminants (Kandemir and Taşkın 2022). Sheep breeds, which are widely bred all over the world, are important in meeting sustainability demands due to their role in organic production (Koyuncu and Taşkın 2016). The Hamdani sheep breed is preferred by breeders due to its milk and fleece yield (Al‐Barzinji et al. 2011) and its ability to adapt well to the southeastern region of Türkiye (İşbilir, İşbilir et al. 2024). Small ruminants are intermediate food animals that shape their grazing and browsing behaviour depending on availability, fineness and food quality (Hofmann 1989). Through grazing, they determine their diet by selecting certain plants and rejecting others (Lamy et al. 2012). Therefore, morphological changes in the tongue are very important for understanding the nature of their diet (Meier et al. 2016). The differences in the shape and distribution of papillae on the tongue mucosa in different ruminants are important for classification in terms of comparative anatomy (Delibaş et al. 2023). In addition, it is thought that determining the development and anatomy of the tongue would be useful for surgical approaches such as partial glossectomy (Lyle 1971; Ducharme 2004), ventral glossectomy (Ducharme 2004; Yong et al. 2008) and full‐thickness glossectomy (Abou‐El‐Ella 1999) in adult animals.

This study aimed to determine the developmental morphology of the tongue and lingual papillae in the foetal period, as well as protein expression in the tongue epithelium at different prenatal developmental stages in Hamdani sheep. Light microscopy was used to observe changes during development and immunohistochemical expression of several proteins involved in epithelial developmental processes, and scanning electron microscopy was used to determine three‐dimensional changes in ultrastructural morphology.

sec.title: Animals
sec.p: Foetuses from pregnant ewes slaughtered in private slaughterhouses in Siirt province were used in our study. The foetuses belonged to animals raised in the same barn under the same conditions. A total of 15 foetuses, 5 from each of the first (40–50 days), second (56–73 days) and third (105–118 days) trimesters, were included in the study. Twin pregnancies were excluded from the study, whereas foetuses from single pregnancies were used. The gestational age of the collected foetuses was calculated using the formula X = 2.1(Y + 17) (X = gestation period (days), Y = crown‐anus length) (Noakes et al. 2001; İşbilir and Güzel 2024). Tongue tissues were carefully dissected from the foetuses and removed from the oral cavity.

sec.title: Macroscopic Examination
sec.p: The dissected tongues were examined macroscopically. The differences observed among the first, second and third trimesters were noted. A stereomicroscope (Olympus SZ61, Japan) was used to examine the first‐trimester tongues.

sec.title: Tissue Processing and Histochemical Analyses
sec.p: The dissected tissue samples were fixed in a 10% buffered formalin solution (pH 7.4) for 24 h. The tissues were then passed through an alcohol/xylene series and embedded in paraffin. Sections of 5‐µm thickness were taken from the paraffin blocks. For histological examination, the sections were stained with Masson's Trichrome and Haematoxylin and Eosin staining methods. The sections were examined and photographed under a light microscope (Olympus BX 50) equipped with a digital camera (Olympus DP71 CCD colour camera).

sec.title: Immunohistochemical Analyses
sec.p: Immunohistochemical analyses were performed on paraffin sections mounted on poly‐l‐lysine‐coated slides (Kastamoni et al. 2023). Deparaffinized sections were passed through an alcohol series. Subsequently, the sections were subjected to antigen retrieval in pH 6.0 sodium citrate buffer at 98°C in an oven for 30 min. To suppress endogenous peroxidase activity, a 3% hydrogen peroxide solution was applied for 10 min. Incubation with 10% horse serum was performed at room temperature to prevent nonspecific binding. Then, the sections were incubated overnight at +4°C with the following primary antibodies: Rabbit anti‐EphB3 (1:600 dilution, Elabscience), Mouse anti‐E‐cadherin (1:100), Mouse anti‐CK‐5 (1:300) and Mouse anti‐EphB2 (1:300). The sections were then incubated with biotin‐conjugated donkey anti‐rabbit or donkey anti‐mouse secondary antibodies (Jackson ImmunoResearch, UK) at a 1:300 dilution for 2 h at room temperature. After ABC complex treatment, the labelled proteins were visualized using DAB chromogen. Counterstaining was performed with Harris Haematoxylin. Cytoplasmic labelling was evaluated across the three trimesters. Sections were examined and photographed under an Olympus BX 50 photomicroscope.

sec.title: Scanning Electron Microscopic (SEM) Examination
sec.p: SEM images were obtained using a scanning electron microscope at the Dicle University Science and Technology Application and Research Centre. After the tissues were dissected, they were placed in a 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution (pH 7.2) for 1 day to complete the first fixation step. They were then washed with 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) at 10‐min intervals, three times. After washing, the second fixation step was completed by treatment with osmium tetroxide for 2 h while rotating in a rotator at room temperature. Thereafter, the tissues were washed again in phosphate buffer three times for 10 min each. Tissues were dehydrated in 25%, 50%, 75% and 90% ethyl alcohol at +4°C for 15 min each. After dehydration, each tissue was kept in 96% and 100% ethyl alcohol for 30 min. The drying phase was completed in an oven at 60°C for 2 days. Finally, gold sputter coating was performed to finalize the preparation (İşbilir, Özgüden Akkoç et al. 2024). After preparation, the tissues were examined under an electron microscope at different magnifications and photographed.

sec.title: Macroscopic Findings and SEM Findings
sec.p: On macroscopic examination, the tongue had an almost flat surface in the first trimester, but in the second trimester, fungiform papillae were observed macroscopically at the apex, unlike in the first trimester. In the last trimester, almost all of the macroscopic structures that can be observed in adult sheep were present (Figure 1). In the first trimester, the apex and dorsum of the tongue were almost flat. There were very few fungiform papillae outlines on the surface of the tongue (Figure 2). Lingual papillae were detected at the root of the tongue. The outline of the circumvallate and lentiform papillae was clearly visible at the root of the tongue. The circumvallate papillae appeared as circular grooves and small projections surrounded by an annular cushion (Figure 2).

caption.p: Dorsal macroscopic view of first‐ (A and A′), second‐ (B and B′) and third‐ (C and C′) trimester foetal tongues.

Macroscopic and SEM analyses of the tongue in the first trimester. General macroscopic image of the tongue in the first trimester (A), apex linguae (B), dorsum linguae (C) and radix linguae (D), red circle: lenticular papillae; red arrow: vallate papillae. SEM analyses of the apex of the tongue in the first trimester (E and F) and SEM analyses of the radix in the first trimester (G and H). White arrowhead: papillary primordium; *: papilla lentiformis and white arrow: papilla circumvallata.

sec.p: In the second trimester, the tongue grew in direct proportion to the development of the organism. Fungiform papillae developed on the epithelial surface at the apex and dorsum of the tongue. Filiform papillae were not yet visible on the epithelial surface (Figure 3). Lentiform papillae covered the entire root of the tongue (Figure 3), except for the area covered by the circumvallate papillae, and were arranged in two rows (4–5 each) on each side (Figure 3).

caption.p: Macroscopic and SEM analyses of the tongue in the second trimester. General macroscopic image of the tongue in the second trimester (A), apex linguae (B), dorsum linguae (C) and radix linguae (D); red circle: lenticular papillae; red arrow: vallate papillae; dashed red arrow: fungiform papillae. SEM analyses of the apex of the tongue in the second trimester (E and F), SEM analyses of the dorsum in the second trimester (G and H) and SEM analyses of the radix in the second trimester (I and J). White arrowhead: fungiform papilla primordium, #: circumvallate papilla primordium.*: white arrow: #: papilla lentiformis, white arrow: papilla circumvallate.

sec.p: In the third trimester, the tongue was well developed and had acquired the adult tongue form with prominent torus linguae. Short and numerous filiform papillae were seen as dome‐like projections on the rostral part of the tongue body and the lateral parts of the tongue tip. Numerous thread‐like epithelial projections were seen between these papillae. Fungiform papillae were clearly visible on the dorsal part of the tongue. The primordia of the filiform papillae completely covered the surface (Figure 4). The torus and root of the tongue were covered with lentiform papillae and circumvallate papillae (Figure 4).

caption.p: Macroscopic and SEM analyses of the tongue in the third trimester. General macroscopic view of the tongue in the third trimester (A), apex linguae (B), dorsum linguae (C) and radix linguae (D), red circle: lenticular papillae; red arrow: vallate papillae; dashed red arrow: fungiform papillae. SEM analyses the apex of the tongue in the third trimester (E and F), SEM analyses the dorsum in the third trimester (G and H) and SEM analyses the radix in the third trimester (I and J). White arrowhead: papilla fungiform; star: papilla filiformis; #: papilla circumvallate.

sec.title: Histochemical Findings
sec.p: In the first trimester, the dorsal and ventral surfaces of the tongue were covered with epithelial tissue. On the dorsal surface, especially at the apex linguae, a single layer of columnar epithelium, probably composed of basal germinative cells, was observed. Towards the root of the tongue, it was observed that the columnar epithelium was covered with 1–2 cell layers and acquired a structure close to non‐keratinized stratified squamous epithelium. Immediately below the epithelium, embryonic connective tissue consisting of small, basophilic‐stained mesenchymal cells and very fine fibres was observed. On the dorsal surface of the tongue, epithelial cell accumulations forming various tongue papillae and invagination‐like formations in the connective tissue were observed. Connective tissue and mesenchymal cell masses invaginated to form primitive papillary primordia. The presence of small capillary structures penetrating towards the papillary epithelium in the connective tissue was also remarkable. The primitive apex and dorsum were found to be filled with structures thought to be fungiform papillae primordia (Figure 5A, black arrow), more filiform (Figure 5A, black arrow) and broader in the apical parts (Figure 5A′). In the root of the tongue, numerous papillary primordia were seen, observed as invaginations with a narrower basal part and a wider apical part. Papillary primordia larger than the apex and dorsum and located on the root were also thought to be primitive circumvallate papillae (Figure 5B,B′).

caption.p: Light microscopic analyses of the tongue in the first and second trimesters. Papillary primordia on the developing apex (A and A′) and radix (B and B′) in the first trimester. Papillary primordia on the apex (C and D) and fungiform papilla primordiums (D and D′) in the second trimester. Papillary primordia on the dorsum (E, F and F′) in the second trimester on the dorsum in the second trimester. Papillary primordia on the radix (G and H), circumvallate papilla primordiums (G) and lenticular papilla primordiums (H and H′). Black arrow: papillary primordia; black arrowhead: papillary primordia of the developing radix. Red arrow: developing taste buds.

sec.p: In the second trimester, the epithelial layers lining the dorsal and ventral surfaces of the tongue were thickened compared to the first trimester. Unlike the first trimester, the underlying connective tissue was differentiated into two regions: loose connective tissue filling the papillae under the epithelium and denser connective tissue extending towards the muscles. Papillary primordia became more prominent with further development. The papillae developing at the apex (Figure 5C,D) and dorsum (Figure 5E,F) of the tongue were more like finger‐like structures. The filiform papillae primordia were shaped as smaller finger‐like structures (Figure 5E,F), and those with larger apical surfaces distributed among them were considered fungiform papillae primordia. The epithelium covering the papillae appeared to be non‐keratinized stratified squamous epithelium consisting of several layers with a prominent stratum basale. In the root of the developing tongue, large papillary outlines were observed, which were thought to be primitive circumvallate papillae (Figure 5G), and primordia, described as oblique finger‐like projections, were thought to be primitive lentiform papillae (Figure 5H). Immature taste bud primordia were observed in the epithelium on the apical surface of some papillary outlines (Figure 5D–G, red arrow). At this stage of development, 1–2 taste buds per papilla section were observed with taste pores and incompletely distinguishable taste cells.

In the third trimester, the epithelial layers lining the dorsal and ventral surfaces of the tongue became thicker compared to the second trimester, with a very thin keratinized layer observed on the taste papillae. The connective tissue was easily distinguished as loose connective tissue under the epithelium and irregular compact connective tissue underneath. Papillae primordia were observed to develop into structures similar to the mature form. There were numerous filiform papillae at the apex of the tongue (Figure 6A,A′). These papillae were covered with keratinized stratified squamous epithelium with a prominent stratum granulosum layer containing keratohyalin granules and a keratin layer. Between these papillae, there were fewer fungiform papillae covered with non‐keratinized stratified squamous epithelium and containing taste buds on the apical surface (Figure 6B,B′). In the more developed papillary connective tissue invaginations, the presence of numerous blood vessels was easily recognized in both types of papillae. Similar to the apex, the dorsum showed numerous filiform papillae and similar histologic features, with fungiform (Figure 6C,C′) and filiform papillae (Figure 6D,D′).

caption.p: Light microscopic analysis of the tongue in the third trimester. In the third trimester, lingual papillae are at the apex (A and B). Filiform (A and A′) and fungiform papillae (B and B′) are like mature forms. In the third trimester, fungiform (C and C′) and filiform (D and D′) papillae mature form on the dorsum (C and D). In the third trimester, lenticular papillae (E and E′) and fungiform papillae (F and F′) are on the torus. In the third trimester, circumvallate papillae (G) like mature forms on the torus, taste bud (G′) and von Ebner gland (H and H′). Black arrow: taste bud; black arrowhead: duct.

sec.p: Light microscopic analysis of the torus linguae revealed the presence of a thickened epithelial layer and numerous lentiform papillae compared to other anatomical regions. The epithelium in this region was more keratinized, especially in the lentiform papillae, and keratohyalin granules were observed (Figure 6E–I). Fungiform papillae with developed taste buds were also observed on the apical surface (Figure 6F,F′). Circumvallate papillae surrounded by a groove were positioned centrally at the apex of the tongue (Figure 6G). These papillae were larger than the other papillae, and their surface was covered with non‐keratinized stratified squamous epithelium. Numerous taste buds were observed on the lateral and apical surface epithelium of the papillae. High magnification examination of the taste buds (Figure 6G′) revealed a taste pore on the apical surface and a distinction between taste cells, identified by paler staining, and support cells, which stained darker. Immature circumvallate papillae, still developing, were also observed in sections. In addition to the circular papillae, conical papillae covered with non‐keratinized stratified squamous epithelium were also observed in the tongue root. Unlike other developmental stages, the tongue root was characterized by the presence of numerous serous glands. These glands were located in the lower parts of the circumvallate papillae, and their excretory ducts opened into the base of the groove of these papillae (Figure 6H,H′).

sec.title: Immunohistochemical Findings
sec.p: Immunohistochemical expression of CK‐5 and E‐cadherin was evaluated at three different prenatal stages (Figure 7). In the first trimester, epithelial cells forming the thin epithelial layer were observed to express CK‐5 and E‐cadherin. CK‐5 expression was concentrated in the flat cells at the apical but not the basal part of the epithelium (Figure 7A), whereas E‐cadherin was more strongly expressed in the cells forming the apex of the papillary primordium (Figure 7B). In the second trimester, CK‐5 and E‐cadherin expression were detected in more cells in the dorsum and root (Figure 7E,F), where epithelial layers were thicker than in the apex (Figure 7C,D). The expression of CK‐5 was more intense in the superficial layers of the epithelium than in the basal layers, although it was evident in the dorsum and root (Figure 7E). In the second trimester, the expression of E‐cadherin was also more prominent in the dorsum and root. E‐cadherin expression throughout the papillary epithelial layer was similar throughout all layers of the epithelium but was stronger in the suprabasal areas (Figure 7F). In the third trimester, the expression of CK‐5 was less intense in taste papillae covered with thin epithelium (Figure 7G) than in mechanical papillae with dense keratinization (Figure 7I). CK‐5 expression was observed to be more intense on the apical surfaces of filiform papillae. In the third trimester, E‐cadherin expression was observed in both taste and mechanical papillae. In highly keratinized filiform and lentiform papillae, E‐cadherin expression was stronger in interpapillary areas (Figure 7J) and less intense on apical surfaces (Figure 7J).

caption.p: Expression of CK‐5 and E‐cadherin in the papillary epithelium during foetal periods. Expression of CK‐5 (A) and E‐cadherin in the epithelium overlying the developing papillary primordium in the first trimester (B). Expression of CK‐5 (C) and E‐cadherin in the thinner stratified epithelium of the developing papillary primordium in the second trimester (D). Expression of CK‐5 (E) and E‐cadherin (F) in the thicker stratified epithelium in the second trimester. Expression of CK‐5 (G) and E‐cadherin (H) in the epithelium overlying the taste papillae in the third trimester, and expression of CK‐5 (I) and E‐cadherin (J) in the epithelium overlying the mechanical papillae.

sec.p: Immunohistochemical expression of EphB2 and EphB3 receptors was evaluated at three different prenatal stages (Figure 8). In the first trimester, the expression of EphB2 was positive throughout the tongue epithelium and was particularly high in the interpapillary areas of the papillary primordia (Figure 8A). Expression of EphB3 was also positive throughout the tongue epithelium, and EphB3 showed a more homogeneously distributed expression pattern throughout the epithelium compared to EphB2 (Figure 8B). In the second trimester, the expression of EphB2 was more intense towards the superficial areas of the thickening epithelial layers (Figure 8C–E), whereas the expression of EphB3 was stronger in the basal cell layer of the epithelial layer (Figure 8D–F). In the third trimester, the expression of EphB2 was more homogeneously distributed throughout the epithelial layers, whereas the expression of EphB3 was more intense in the basal cells, similar to the second trimester. These results suggest that EphB2 and EphB3 receptors may play a role in the development of the tongue epithelium of Hamdani sheep.

caption.p: Expression of EphB2 and EphB3 in papillary epithelium during foetal periods. Expression of EphB2 in the epithelium overlying developing papillary primordium in the first trimester; note the interpapillary areas (A). Expression of EphB3 in the epithelium of the tongue in the first trimester (B). Expression of EphB2 (C) and EphB3 (D) in the epithelium of the more finger‐like developing papillary primordium in the second trimester. Expression of EphB2 (E) and EphB3 (F) in the epithelium of the wider papillary primordium in the second trimester. Expression of EphB2 (G and I) and EphB3 in mechanical papillae that are similar to mature in the third trimester (H–J).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study was conducted to investigate the developmental changes in the tongue and papillae during the three trimesters of prenatal life in sheep foetuses. Although morphological studies have been carried out in the past in different sheep breeds at different periods, our study is the first to investigate the morphological developmental changes in the papillae during the three trimesters in sheep foetuses, and the investigation also contains several molecular clues.

In goat foetuses at 38 days of age, it has been reported that the tongue consists of two prominent lateral swellings and contains epithelium, mesenchymal cells, differentiating myocytes, differentiating leukocytes and numerous blood vessels. Consistent with the first‐trimester finding of our study, the dorsal surface of the tongue has been reported to be covered with two to three layers of epithelial tissue in different goat foetuses (between 50 and 60 days) (Igbokwe and Okolic 2009; Chudasama et al. 2016). Experimental studies (Frederick et al. 1994) show that mechanical papillae are the first to differentiate, whereas taste papillae appear close to birth. According to our results, it is possible to say that mechanical and taste papillae begin to develop from the first trimester in sheep. Moreover, our results are similar to the study on camels in terms of the order and timing of the appearance of papillae (Abou‐Elhamd et al. 2018). Singh et al. (2021) could not distinguish filiform, fungiform and circumvallate papillae in goats before 50 days. In our study, it was possible to distinguish all three papillae by light microscopy in the first trimester.

In a scanning electron microscopy study in rabbits, no rudiments of filiform papillae were observed in the 18‐day‐old foetus, whereas in the 26‐day‐old foetus, the rudiments of filiform papillae were compact and densely distributed along the entire dorsal surface of the vertex, trunk and tongue margins (Kulawik and Godynicki 2013). In our study, filiform papillae were similar to those found in camels during the foetal period. In the first trimester, these papillae were observed in the primordium, and in the second trimester, they were observed to be distributed along the apex and dorsum (Abou‐Elhamd et al. 2018).

In our study, fungiform papillae were observed as a primordium in the first trimester of pregnancy but gradually differentiated in the second and third trimesters. As observed in cattle (Chamorro et al. 1986), Akkaraman sheep (Ünsal et al. 2003), deer (Zheng and Kobayashi 2006) and camels (Sharifabad and Salehi 2014), immature taste bud primordia were observed in the apical parts of fungiform papillae in the second trimester, whereas these structures developed in the third trimester.

In a scanning electron microscopy study in sheep, it was reported that in the second trimester, no base of the circumvallate papillae could be distinguished, and there was no evidence of taste pores in the epithelium that would indicate the presence of differentiated taste buds, and only in the third trimester did circumvallate and fungiform papillae contain taste buds (Tichy 1992). In our study, electron microscopic examination showed that the papillae that appeared as small protrusions surrounded by circular grooves and an annular cushion in the first trimester were circumvallate papillae. Similarly, the large papillary primordium observed in the tongue root in the first trimester on light microscopy was thought to be a primitive circumvallate papilla. Moreover, in the second trimester, primitive taste bud primordia were detected in the apical parts of these papillae. In the third trimester, in addition to the still‐developing papillae, taste buds on the lateral and apical surfaces and an opening area on the apical surface were identified. These findings are consistent with previous studies in camels in which taste buds were identified on the lateral wall of circumvallate papillae in the second trimester and on both the dorsal and lateral surfaces in the third trimester (Abou‐Elhamd et al. 2018). However, Ebner's glands, which are located close to the circumvallate papillae and play a role in washing the vallum (Sbarbati et al. 1999), have been identified in the deeper parts of the circumvallate papillae in the third trimester in camels (Abou‐Elhamd et al. 2018). The presence of numerous serous glands at the root of the tongue, in the lower parts of the circumvallate papillae, and the opening of the drainage ducts to the base of the papillae in the third‐trimester sheep tongue supports this information.

There is information that lenticular papillae did not develop macroscopically and light microscopically in Awassi sheep foetuses during the first and second trimesters of pregnancy (Ali and Al‐Jebori 2024). In a study on goats, papillae were first noticed on Day 127 of gestation and were located on the rostral and dorsolateral sides of the torus linguae. Lenticular papillae were observed as a slightly raised, roughened area on the torus on Day 112 of gestation, whereas lenticular papillae were clearly distinguished between Days 121 and 127 (Arti et al. 2021). In the present study, lenticular papillae started to form in the first trimester and were well‐developed in the third trimester.

The expression of CK has been generally characterized in human tissues. Although normal human adult ventral tongue epithelium expresses CK 4, 5, 6, 13, 14 and 16, dorsal tongue epithelium is known to express CK 1, 5, 6, 10, 14 and 16 (Morgan and Su 1994). In our study, similar to human samples, we found the expression of CK‐5 more prominently on the papillae in the dorsum and torus linguae, where the epithelial layers began to thicken more starting from the second trimester. However, we also identified the expression of CK‐5 in the epithelium in all tongue segments in the last trimester in sheep tissue.

Although there are many reports on the role of Eph proteins in the development of other epithelial tissues and the nervous system, there is only one study of Eph receptors in tongue development. In a study with mouse embryos, it was reported that EphBs, especially EphB1 and EphB2, are expressed in the tongue and that Eph/ephrin signalling is required for normal innervation of the fungiform papillae (Treffy et al. 2016). In another aspect, the literature regarding the expression of the Eph receptor family in sheep tissues is limited to only one study. In this study, the expression of the EphA4 receptor and its ligand EphrinA3 in the development and morphogenesis of hair follicles in fine wool sheep was determined (Cui et al. 2022). Our results show that EphB2 and EphB3 receptors are expressed in the epithelium during papillae development in the sheep tongue. Our results provide important evidence supporting the presence of expression of Eph receptors in a different animal species, both in terms of mammalian tongue development and also in a different tissue in sheep. Furthermore, the results of an experimental study reported that in mammalian epithelial tissues, the interaction of the EphB3 receptor and ligand ephrin B1 triggers ADAM10‐mediated cleavage of the cell adhesion protein E‐cadherin and plays a role in cellular adhesion and differentiation during epithelial development (Solanas et al. 2011). According to our findings, especially in the second trimester, EphB3 showed intense expression in the basal region, whereas E‐cadherin was found to be concentrated in the suprabasal areas immediately adjacent to it. The expression pattern of these two proteins may be understood in future studies as playing a fundamental role in the development of the tongue papillae in sheep or any mammal.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Studying the development of the epithelium of the tongue in detail is important for understanding developmental language defects in various animal species and humans, as well as for understanding species‐specific epithelial‐cell interactions at the molecular level. The results of the study reveal macroscopic and microscopic features of tongue development in sheep foetuses during the first, second and third trimesters of gestation. It was observed that papillary development started as early stage structures in the first trimester of pregnancy and became closer to the postnatal period in the third trimester. In addition, epithelial markers such as CK‐5 and E‐cadherin were identified for the first time in sheep during all three foetal periods, and histochemical results also showed that keratinization had developed. Considering the previously described molecular relationship between E‐cadherin and the EphB3 receptor in the literature and their localization in our findings, it may provide a theoretical basis for the study of the mechanisms of papillary development in the future.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Barış Can Güzel: conceptualization, investigation, supervision, writing – original draft, writing – review and editing. Fatma İşbilir: writing – review and editing, visualization, supervision, conceptualization. İhsan Işbilir: conceptualization, investigation, visualization. Senem Esin Yavaş: conceptualization, investigation, supervision, writing – original draft, writing – review and editing.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: With the ethics committee report numbered 2024/05/23, the Siirt University Experimental Animals Application and Research Centre approved the procedures used in our investigation.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: Peer Review
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://www.webofscience.com/api/gateway/wos/peer‐review/10.1002/vms3.70403.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study can be requested from the corresponding author.
